threadName: Using OKRs for GitHub release management

messages.content: on github when i want to do version managment how can i use Automated release notes

Here's how to use **Automated Release Notes** on GitHub for version management:

## Creating Automated Release Notes

1. Navigate to your repository → click **Releases** → **Draft a new release**.
2. Choose or create a **tag** (e.g., `v1.0.0`) and select the target branch.
3. Optionally select a **Previous tag** to define the range of changes.
4. Click **Generate release notes** — GitHub will auto-populate the description with merged pull requests and contributors since the previous tag.
5. Review, edit if needed, and click **Publish release**.

## Customizing with `.github/release.yml`

Create a `.github/release.yml` file to organize and filter your release notes:

```yaml name=.github/release.yml
changelog:
  exclude:
    labels:
      - ignore-for-release
    authors:
      - octocat
  categories:
    - title: Breaking Changes 🛠
      labels:
        - Semver-Major
        - breaking-change
    - title: Exciting New Features 🎉
      labels:
        - Semver-Minor
        - enhancement
    - title: 🐛 Bug Fixes
      labels:
        - bug
    - title: Other Changes
      labels:
        - "*"
```

### Key configuration options

| Parameter | Description |
|---|---|
| `changelog.exclude.labels` | Labels that exclude a PR from release notes |
| `changelog.exclude.authors` | Users/bots to exclude |
| `changelog.categories[*].title` | Category heading in the notes |
| `changelog.categories[*].labels` | Labels that qualify a PR for the category (`*` = catch-all) |

## Tips for Version Management

- **Use semantic versioning tags** (e.g., `v1.0.0`, `v1.1.0`) so GitHub can auto-detect the "latest" release.
- **Label your PRs consistently** (e.g., `enhancement`, `bug`, `breaking-change`) — the generated notes will group them by your configured categories.
- **Exclude noise** by filtering out bot authors (like Dependabot) or internal labels.

For full details, see [Automatically generated release notes](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/releasing-projects-on-github/automatically-generated-release-notes).

references.query: how can i use Automated release notes for version management on GitHub

results.title: Automatically generated release notes
results.content:
/en/repositories/releasing-projects-on-github/automatically-generated-release-notes Automatically generated release notes Automatically generated release notes:  # Automatically generated release notes

You can automatically generate release notes for your GitHub releases

## About automatically generated release notes

Automatically generated release notes provide an automated alternative to manually writing release notes for your GitHub releases. With automatically generated release notes, you can quickly generate an overview of the contents of a release. Automatically generated release notes include a list of merged pull requests, a list of contributors to the release, and a link to a full changelog.

You can also customize your automated release notes, using labels to create custom categories to organize pull requests you want to include, and exclude certain labels and users from appearing in the output.

## Creating automatically generated release notes for a new release

1. On GitHub, navigate to the main page of the repository.
2. To the right of the list of files, click **Releases**.

   ![Screenshot of the main page of a repository. A link, labeled "Releases", is highlighted with an orange outline.](/assets/images/help/releases/release-link.png)
3. At the top of the page, click **Draft a new release**.
4. To choose a tag for the release, select the **Choose a tag** dropdown menu.
   * To use an existing tag, click the tag.
   * To create a new tag, type a version number for your release, then click **Create new tag**.
5. If you created a new tag, select the **Target** dropdown menu, then click the branch that contains the project you want to release.
6. Optionally, above the description field, select the **Previous tag** dropdown menu, then click the tag that identifies the previous release.

   ![Screenshot of the "New release" form. A dropdown menu, labeled "Previous tag: auto", is highlighted with an orange outline.](/assets/images/help/releases/releases-tag-previous-release.png)
7. In the "Release title" field, type a title for your release.
8. Above the description field, click **Generate release notes**.
9. Check the generated notes to ensure they include all (and only) the information you want to include.
10. Optionally, to include binary files such as compiled programs in your release, drag and drop or manually select files in the binaries box.
11. Optionally, to notify users that the release is not ready for production and may be unstable, select **This is a pre-release**.
12. Optionally, select **Set as latest release**. If you do not select this option, the latest release label will automatically be assigned based on semantic versioning.
13. Optionally, if GitHub Discussions is enabled for the repository, create a discussion for the release.
    * Select **Create a discussion for this release**.
    * Select the **Category** dropdown menu, then click a category for the release discussion.
14. If you're ready to publicize your release, click **Publish release**. To work on the release later, click **Save draft**. If you have enabled immutable releases for the repository, creating a draft first allows you to attach all assets before the release becomes immutable.
    You can then view your published or draft releases in the releases feed for your repository. For more information, see [Viewing your repository's releases and tags](/en/repositories/releasing-projects-on-github/viewing-your-repositorys-releases-and-tags).

## Configuring automatically generated release notes

1. On GitHub, navigate to the main page of the repository.
2. Above the list of files, select the **Add file** [icon: The downwards-facing triangle icon] dropdown menu, then click **[icon: plus] Create new file**.

   Alternatively, you can click [icon: The plus sign icon] in the file tree view on the left.

   ![Screenshot of the main page of a repository highlighting both the "Add file" and the "plus sign" icon, described above, with an orange outline.](/assets/images/help/repository/add-file-buttons.png)
3. In the file name field, type `.github/release.yml`. This will create a new file called `release.yml` in the `.github` directory.
4. In the file, using the configuration options below, specify in YAML the pull request labels and authors you want to exclude from this release. You can also create new categories and list the pull request labels to be included in each of them.

### Configuration options

| Parameter                                 | Description                                                                                                                                                    |
| :---------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `changelog.exclude.labels`                | A list of labels that exclude a pull request from appearing in release notes.                                                                                  |
| `changelog.exclude.authors`               | A list of user or bot login handles whose pull requests are to be excluded from release notes.                                                                 |
| `changelog.categories[*].title`           | **Required.** The title of a category of changes in release notes.                                                                                             |
| `changelog.categories[*].labels`          | **Required.** Labels that qualify a pull request for this category. Use `*` as a catch-all for pull requests that didn't match any of the previous categories. |
| `changelog.categories[*].exclude.labels`  | A list of labels that exclude a pull request from appearing in this category.                                                                                  |
| `changelog.categories[*].exclude.authors` | A list of user or bot login handles whose pull requests are to be excluded from this category.                                                                 |

### Example configurations

A configuration for a repository that labels semver releases

```yaml copy
# .github/release.yml

changelog:
  exclude:
    labels:
      - ignore-for-release
    authors:
      - octocat
  categories:
    - title: Breaking Changes 🛠
      labels:
        - Semver-Major
        - breaking-change
    - title: Exciting New Features 🎉
      labels:
        - Semver-Minor
        - enhancement
    - title: Other Changes
      labels:
        - "*"
```

A configuration for a repository that doesn't tag pull requests but where we want to separate out Dependabot automated pull requests in release notes (`labels: '*'` is required to display a catchall category)

```yaml copy
# .github/release.yml

changelog:
  categories:
    - title: 🏕 Features
      labels:
        - '*'
      exclude:
        labels:
          - dependencies
    - title: 👒 Dependencies
      labels:
        - dependencies
```

## Further reading

* [Managing labels](/en/issues/using-labels-and-milestones-to-track-work/managing-labels)

results.title: Release note content type
results.content:
/en/contributing/style-guide-and-content-model/release-note-content-type Release note content type Release note content type:  # Release note content type

Release notes enable readers to understand and prepare for the user-facing changes in each release of GitHub's versioned enterprise products.

Good release notes provide administrators the necessary information to plan system upgrades in environments that require change control, and support end users who want to understand and prepare to use new GitHub features and functionality.

Writers source, edit, and publish release notes in collaboration with product DRIs, feature owners, and individual engineers at GitHub. For each individual release, we group release notes by predefined types.

We publish the release notes for [GitHub Enterprise Server](/en/enterprise-server@3.20/admin/release-notes) on GitHub Docs, in the "Enterprise administrators" documentation set.

## Types of releases

GitHub Docs provides release notes for feature releases and patch releases of GitHub Enterprise Server. For more information, see [About upgrades to new releases](/en/enterprise-server@3.20/admin/overview/about-upgrades-to-new-releases) in the GitHub Enterprise Server documentation.

## Guidance and example release notes

You can review guidance for the format, style, and tone of release notes, as well as examples of each type of note. For more information, see [Style guide](/en/contributing/style-guide-and-content-model/style-guide#release-notes).

results.title: Style guide
results.content:
/en/contributing/style-guide-and-content-model/style-guide Style guide Style guide:  # documentation.
```

To insert this disclaimer, use the `{% data reusables.actions.actions-not-certified-by-github-comment %}` reusable.

For GitHub Docs purposes, a third-party action is any action that doesn't have the `actions/`, `github/` or `octo-org/` prefix. For example, this is a first-party action:

```yaml
steps:
  - uses: actions/checkout@main
```

This is an example of a third-party action:

```yaml
steps:
    - uses: google-github-actions/setup-gcloud@1bee7de035d65ec5da40a31f8589e240eba8fde5
```

Examples:

* See the code block in [Publishing to package registries](/en/actions/guides/building-and-testing-python#publishing-to-package-registries)

#### Pinning version numbers to SHA

Code examples that use third-party actions must always pin to a full length commit SHA, instead of the version number or branch:

```yaml
steps:
    - uses: google-github-actions/setup-gcloud@1bee7de035d65ec5da40a31f8589e240eba8fde5
```

For GitHub Docs purposes, a third-party action is any action that doesn't have one of the following prefixes: `actions/`, `github/`, and `octo-org/`. For example, this is a first-party action:

```yaml
steps:
  - uses: actions/javascript-action@main
```

For more information, see [Using SHAs](/en/actions/learn-github-actions/finding-and-customizing-actions#using-shas)

### Codespaces

When referring to the product Codespaces, always include "GitHub", except in these circumstances:

* In the `shortTitle` front matter.
* In subheadings within an article, if "Codespaces" has already been used anywhere in the article prior to the subheading.

Variables: `{% data variables.product.prodname_github_codespaces %}` ("GitHub Codespaces") and `{% data variables.product.prodname_codespaces %}` ("Codespaces").

When referring to instances of remote working environments created with this technology, refer to these as "codespaces" (lowercase c). For example, "to delete your codespace" or "to list your codespaces."

Always use "dev container" (or, where clarification is needed, its longer form "development container") and not "devcontainer" (one word), except in file/path names. The single word could form could be considered a brand, which we want to avoid, and we also want to be consistent with the two-word form used in [the Visual Studio Code documentation](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/remote/create-dev-container#_path-to-creating-a-dev-container).

Use "development container configuration files" to refer to all of the files in the `.devcontainer` directory (plus the `.devcontainer.json` if that's being used rather than `devcontainer.json` in the `.devcontainer` directory). Don't refer to these as "development container files" or "devcontainer files" to avoid this being taken as referring to `devcontainer.json` files. "Development container configuration files" refers to all of the files that can be used to configure a dev container, including `Dockerfile` and `compose.yaml` files. Don't use "the development container configuration file" (singular) when referring specifically to a `devcontainer.json` file. Instead refer to this file by its name.

### GitHub Advanced Security products (GHAS)

Use the terms `licenses` and `active committers` when you refer to GitHub Advanced Security, GitHub Code Security, or GitHub Secret Protection billing.

We used to use the term `seats` to describe the number of accounts that can use GitHub Advanced Security, GitHub Code Security, or GitHub Secret Protection in an enterprise. People can be confused by the term `seats`, so we removed this term from GitHub.com in autumn 2022 and versions from GHES 3.7 onward do not use it.

### Personal access tokens

GitHub has two types of personal access tokens:

* Fine-grained personal access tokens: Offer granular control over repository access and permissions
* Personal access token (classic): Use scopes and grant access to all repositories that the token owner can access

You should use variables to refer to these types of tokens, as well as to personal access tokens in general:

* Use `{% data variables.product.pat_generic %}`or `{% data variables.product.pat_generic_caps %}` to refer to personal access token in general. Use `{% data variables.product.pat_generic_title_case %}` if the phrase should be in title case ("Personal Access Token") in order to match UI text.
* Use `{% data variables.product.pat_v2 %}` or `{% data variables.product.pat_v2_caps %}` to refer to fine-grained personal access tokens.
* Use `{% data variables.product.pat_v1 %}`, `{% data variables.product.pat_v1_plural %}`, `{% data variables.product.pat_v1_caps %}`, or `{% data variables.product.pat_v1_caps_plural %}` to refer to personal access token (classic).

For more information about GitHub's personal access tokens, see [Managing your personal access tokens](/en/authentication/keeping-your-account-and-data-secure/creating-a-personal-access-token#about-personal-access-tokens).

## Punctuation

Follow standard American English punctuation rules. For more guidance, see “[Punctuation](https://docs.microsoft.com/style-guide/punctuation)” in the Microsoft Style Guide.

## Release notes

A set of release notes on GitHub Docs tell readers about administrator- or user-facing changes to a versioned release of a product like GitHub Enterprise Server (GHES). Release notes appear in the [Release notes](/en/enterprise-server@3.20/admin/release-notes).

A good release note is a few sentences that sequentially answer the reader's questions about the change. For more information, see [Release note content type](/en/contributing/style-guide-and-content-model/release-note-content-type).

Each release note in a set describes one of the following changes.

* [Features](#features): brand-new behavior or functionality
* [Security fixes](#security-fixes): fixes to flaws or unexpected behavior that have security implications
* [Bug fixes](#bug-fixes): fixes to flaws or unexpected behavior
* [Changes](#changes): notable changes to past behavior
* [Known issues](#known-issues): issues that GitHub has identified, but cannot or has not yet prioritized
* [Closing down](#closing-down): the process of being retired and should no longer be relied upon for future work
* [Retired](#retired): end of a product or feature lifecycle

skillExecutions.arguments: {"rawUserQuery": "how can i use Automated release notes for version management on GitHub"}

    * Select **Create a discussion for this release**.

| `changelog.categories[*].exclude.labels`  | A list of labels that exclude a pull request from appearing in this category.                                                                                  |

messages.content: On the Scale of Github Docs, and Release&Change managment how could i use OKR (Objective & Key Results)

# Using OKRs for GitHub-Based Release & Change Management

## What Are OKRs?

- **Objective**: A qualitative, ambitious goal (the *what*)
- **Key Results**: 2–5 measurable outcomes that prove you achieved it (the *how*)

---

## Example OKRs for Release & Change Management

### 🎯 Objective 1: Improve Release Quality & Predictability

| Key Result | How to Measure on GitHub |
|---|---|
| Reduce hotfix releases by 30% | Track tags/releases labeled as hotfix |
| 95% of releases include automated release notes | Check releases with auto-generated changelogs |
| Zero critical bugs escape to production per quarter | Monitor issues labeled `critical` + `escaped-defect` |
| Every release has a corresponding milestone at 100% | Use GitHub Milestones to track completion |

### 🎯 Objective 2: Streamline Change Management Process

| Key Result | How to Measure on GitHub |
|---|---|
| Average PR review time < 24 hours | Track PR open → first review timestamps |
| 100% of PRs are categorized with labels (`bug`, `feature`, `breaking-change`) | Enforce via CI or branch protection rules |
| All releases follow semantic versioning | Audit tag naming conventions |
| Reduce mean time from PR merge to release by 40% | Compare merge dates to release publish dates |

### 🎯 Objective 3: Build a World-Class Documentation Culture

| Key Result | How to Measure on GitHub |
|---|---|
| 100% of new features have a linked docs PR before release | Require docs label or linked PR in templates |
| Docs site has < 5 open `stale` issues at any time | Track with GitHub Issues + stale bot |
| Every release note is reviewed and published within 24h of release | Monitor release timestamps |
| Increase community docs contributions by 25% | Track PRs from external contributors |

---

## Implementing OKRs with GitHub Features

### 1. **GitHub Milestones** → Track Key Results per release cycle
- Create a milestone per version (e.g., `v2.1.0`)
- Attach issues and PRs that map to your Key Results
- Track % completion as a progress indicator

### 2. **GitHub Projects (Boards)** → Visualize OKR progress
- Create a Project board per OKR cycle (e.g., Q2 2026)
- Add columns: `Not Started` → `In Progress` → `Done`
- Use custom fields to tag items by Objective

### 3. **Labels** → Categorize work by OKR
- Create labels like `okr:release-quality`, `okr:docs`, `okr:change-mgmt`
- Use them to filter and report on what work supports which Objective

### 4. **Issue & PR Templates** → Enforce OKR alignment
```markdown name=.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/feature.yml
name: Feature Request
description: Propose a new feature
body:
  - type: dropdown
    id: okr-alignment
    attributes:
      label: Which OKR does this support?
      options:
        - "Release Quality & Predictability"
        - "Streamline Change Management"
        - "Documentation Culture"
        - "Other / Not Applicable"
    validations:
      required: true
```

### 5. **Automated Release Notes** → Feed into Key Results
- Use `.github/release.yml` categories (as discussed earlier) to auto-group changes
- Map categories directly to OKR themes (features → growth OKR, bugs → quality OKR)

### 6. **GitHub Actions** → Automate OKR measurement
- Build workflows that count PRs by label, measure cycle times, or flag missing docs
- Publish OKR dashboards or reports on a schedule

---

## Putting It All Together — A Quarterly Cycle

```
1. Quarter Start   → Define OKRs, create GitHub Project board
2. Sprint/Weekly   → Label issues/PRs by OKR, track in milestones
3. Each Release    → Auto-generate release notes, verify KR targets
4. Quarter End     → Score KRs (0.0–1.0), retrospect, set next OKRs
```

> **Pro tip:** A score of **0.6–0.7** per Key Result is considered healthy — it means you set ambitious but not impossible goals.

---

The key idea: **GitHub is already generating the data** (PRs, issues, releases, labels, milestones). OKRs give you the *framework* to turn that data into meaningful goals and measurable outcomes.

## Implementierung von OKRs mit GitHub-Funktionen

### 1. **GitHub Milestones** → Key Results pro Release-Zyklus verfolgen
- Erstelle einen Milestone pro Version (z. B. `v2.1.0`)
- Verknüpfe Issues und PRs, die auf deine Key Results einzahlen
- Verfolge die prozentuale Fertigstellung als Fortschrittsindikator

### 2. **GitHub Projects (Boards)** → OKR-Fortschritt visualisieren
- Erstelle ein Project Board pro OKR-Zyklus (z. B. Q2 2026)
- Füge Spalten hinzu: `Nicht begonnen` → `In Bearbeitung` → `Erledigt`
- Verwende benutzerdefinierte Felder, um Einträge nach Objective zu kennzeichnen

### 3. **Labels** → Arbeit nach OKR kategorisieren
- Erstelle Labels wie `okr:release-qualität`, `okr:doku`, `okr:change-mgmt`
- Nutze sie zum Filtern und Berichten, welche Arbeit welches Objective unterstützt

### 4. **Issue- & PR-Vorlagen** → OKR-Ausrichtung sicherstellen
```markdown name=.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/feature.yml
name: Feature-Anfrage
description: Schlage ein neues Feature vor
body:
  - type: dropdown
    id: okr-ausrichtung
    attributes:
      label: Welches OKR wird hierdurch unterstützt?
      options:
        - "Release-Qualität & Vorhersagbarkeit"
        - "Change Management optimieren"
        - "Dokumentationskultur"
        - "Sonstiges / Nicht zutreffend"
    validations:
      required: true
```

### 5. **Automatisierte Release Notes** → Key Results speisen
- Verwende `.github/release.yml`-Kategorien, um Änderungen automatisch zu gruppieren
- Ordne Kategorien direkt OKR-Themen zu (Features → Wachstums-OKR, Bugs → Qualitäts-OKR)

### 6. **GitHub Actions** → OKR-Messung automatisieren
- Erstelle Workflows, die PRs nach Label zählen, Durchlaufzeiten messen oder fehlende Dokumentation melden
- Veröffentliche OKR-Dashboards oder Berichte nach Zeitplan
jetzt gib mier von diesen möglichen implementierungen die 3 relevantesten in einer "Project Managment Lense"

## Die 3 relevantesten Implementierungen aus Projektmanagement-Sicht

### 1. **GitHub Projects (Boards)** → OKR-Fortschritt visualisieren

Das ist das Herzstück des Projektmanagements. Boards geben dir:
- **Transparenz** über den aktuellen Stand aller Objectives
- **Priorisierung** durch Spalten und benutzerdefinierte Felder
- **Stakeholder-Kommunikation** — jeder sieht auf einen Blick, wo das Team steht

> 💡 *Warum #1?* Ohne Sichtbarkeit gibt es kein Projektmanagement. Das Board ist dein Single Source of Truth.

---

### 2. **GitHub Milestones** → Key Results pro Release-Zyklus verfolgen

Milestones verbinden OKRs direkt mit lieferbaren Ergebnissen:
- **Zeitliche Verankerung** — jeder Milestone hat ein Fälligkeitsdatum
- **Fortschrittsmessung** — prozentuale Fertigstellung zeigt, ob ihr auf Kurs seid
- **Scope Management** — klar definiert, was in einen Release gehört und was nicht

> 💡 *Warum #2?* Milestones beantworten die Kernfrage: "Liefern wir das Richtige zum richtigen Zeitpunkt?"

---

### 3. **Issue- & PR-Vorlagen** → OKR-Ausrichtung sicherstellen

Vorlagen sind der Governance-Mechanismus im Alltag:
- **Konsistenz** — jede Anfrage wird strukturiert erfasst
- **Strategische Ausrichtung** — Teams müssen explizit angeben, welches OKR unterstützt wird
- **Entscheidungsgrundlage** — Arbeit ohne OKR-Bezug wird sichtbar und kann hinterfragt werden

> 💡 *Warum #3?* Vorlagen verhindern, dass Arbeit entsteht, die keinem Ziel dient — das größte Risiko im Projektmanagement.

---

### Zusammenfassung

| Rang | Funktion | PM-Kernfrage |
|------|----------|--------------|
| 1 | Projects (Boards) | *Wo stehen wir?* |
| 2 | Milestones | *Sind wir auf Kurs?* |
| 3 | Issue-/PR-Vorlagen | *Arbeiten wir am Richtigen?* |

# Semantic Versioning (SemVer) auf GitHub

## Was ist Semantic Versioning?

Das Format ist: **`MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`**

| Segment | Wann erhöhen? | Beispiel |
|---------|---------------|----------|
| **MAJOR** | Breaking Changes — API-Inkompatibilität | `1.0.0` → `2.0.0` |
| **MINOR** | Neue Features — rückwärtskompatibel | `1.0.0` → `1.1.0` |
| **PATCH** | Bugfixes — rückwärtskompatibel | `1.0.0` → `1.0.1` |

Zusätzliche Formate:
- **Pre-release**: `v2.0.0-beta.1`, `v1.1.0-rc.1`
- **Build-Metadata**: `v1.0.0+build.123`

---

## 1. Git Tags für Versionierung verwenden

```bash
# Neuen Tag erstellen
git tag v1.0.0

# Annotated Tag (empfohlen — enthält Autor, Datum, Nachricht)
git tag -a v1.2.0 -m "Release v1.2.0: neue Dashboard-Features"

# Tag auf GitHub pushen
git push origin v1.2.0

# Alle Tags pushen
git push origin --tags
```

---

## 2. GitHub Releases mit SemVer

1. Gehe zu **Releases** → **Draft a new release**
2. Wähle oder erstelle einen Tag im Format `vMAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`
3. Setze den **Previous tag**, um den Änderungsbereich zu definieren
4. Klicke **Generate release notes** für automatische Changelogs
5. Markiere ggf. **Pre-release** für Beta-/RC-Versionen

---

## 3. Labels zur Versionsentscheidung nutzen

Erstelle Labels, die direkt auf SemVer-Segmente abbilden:

| Label | Bedeutung | Version-Auswirkung |
|-------|-----------|---------------------|
| `semver:major` | Breaking Change | MAJOR +1 |
| `semver:minor` | Neues Feature | MINOR +1 |
| `semver:patch` | Bugfix | PATCH +1 |

Diese Labels können in `.github/release.yml` genutzt werden:

```yaml name=.github/release.yml
changelog:
  categories:
    - title: "⚠️ Breaking Changes"
      labels:
        - semver:major
    - title: "🚀 Neue Features"
      labels:
        - semver:minor
    - title: "🐛 Bugfixes"
      labels:
        - semver:patch
    - title: "📦 Sonstige Änderungen"
      labels:
        - "*"
```

---

## 4. Branch-Strategie für SemVer

```
main (stable)
 ├── release/v1.x    ← Wartung für v1
 ├── release/v2.x    ← Wartung für v2
 ├── feature/*        ← neue Features (→ MINOR)
 ├── fix/*            ← Bugfixes (→ PATCH)
 └── breaking/*       ← Breaking Changes (→ MAJOR)
```

### Branch-Protection konfigurieren
- **`main`**: Nur über PR mit Review, alle Checks müssen bestehen
- **`release/*`**: Nur Patch-Commits erlaubt, kein Feature-Merge

---

## 5. Automatisierung mit GitHub Actions

### Automatische Version bestimmen anhand von Labels

```yaml name=.github/workflows/release.yml
name: Semantic Release
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [closed]
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  version:
    if: github.event.pull_request.merged == true
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Version bestimmen
        id: version
        run: |
          LABELS='${{ toJSON(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name) }}'

          # Aktuelle Version aus letztem Tag lesen
          CURRENT=$(git describe --tags --abbrev=0 2>/dev/null || echo "v0.0.0")
          MAJOR=$(echo $CURRENT | cut -d. -f1 | tr -d 'v')
          MINOR=$(echo $CURRENT | cut -d. -f2)
          PATCH=$(echo $CURRENT | cut -d. -f3)

          if echo "$LABELS" | grep -q "semver:major"; then
            MAJOR=$((MAJOR + 1)); MINOR=0; PATCH=0
          elif echo "$LABELS" | grep -q "semver:minor"; then
            MINOR=$((MINOR + 1)); PATCH=0
          else
            PATCH=$((PATCH + 1))
          fi

          echo "new_version=v${MAJOR}.${MINOR}.${PATCH}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Tag erstellen und pushen
        run: |
          git tag ${{ steps.version.outputs.new_version }}
          git push origin ${{ steps.version.outputs.new_version }}

      - name: GitHub Release erstellen
        uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
        with:
          tag_name: ${{ steps.version.outputs.new_version }}
          generate_release_notes: true
```

---

## 6. SemVer in PR-Vorlagen erzwingen

```markdown name=.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md
## Version Impact

Bitte eines der folgenden Labels hinzufügen:

- [ ] `semver:major` — Breaking Change
- [ ] `semver:minor` — Neues Feature (rückwärtskompatibel)
- [ ] `semver:patch` — Bugfix (rückwärtskompatibel)

## Änderungsbeschreibung
<!-- Was wurde geändert und warum? -->
```

### Label-Pflicht per GitHub Action erzwingen

```yaml name=.github/workflows/require-semver-label.yml
name: SemVer Label prüfen
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, labeled, unlabeled, synchronize]

jobs:
  check-label:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: SemVer-Label vorhanden?
        run: |
          LABELS='${{ toJSON(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name) }}'
          if echo "$LABELS" | grep -qE "semver:(major|minor|patch)"; then
            echo "✅ SemVer-Label gefunden"
          else
            echo "❌ Bitte ein semver:major, semver:minor oder semver:patch Label hinzufügen"
            exit 1
          fi
```

---

## Zusammenfassung — SemVer Workflow auf GitHub

```
1. PR erstellen        → Branch-Name: feature/*, fix/*, breaking/*
2. Label setzen        → semver:major / semver:minor / semver:patch
3. Review & Merge      → Branch Protection erzwingt Review + Label-Check
4. Automatische Action → Bestimmt neue Version, erstellt Tag + Release
5. Release Notes       → Automatisch gruppiert nach SemVer-Kategorie
```

| Schritt | GitHub-Feature |
|---------|----------------|
| Version definieren | Git Tags (`v1.2.3`) |
| Version kategorisieren | Labels (`semver:*`) |
| Version erzwingen | Branch Protection + Actions |
| Version dokumentieren | Automated Release Notes |
| Version veröffentlichen | GitHub Releases |
